prompt: A monumental wall representing the passage of time, composed of tightly stacked layers like geological strata, viewed in perspective so that it stretches from deep background (past) to extreme foreground (present). The far end of the wall (past decades) is dense, stable, and cold — smooth stone in muted blue-grey tones, perfectly aligned and structurally sound. As the wall approaches the foreground, the layers progressively deform: surfaces fracture, edges warp, and the material transitions into brittle, overheated textures glowing from within with deep red and molten orange. The most recent layers at the front are violently collapsing outward toward the viewer, breaking apart mid-air into fragments and liquid-like drips, as if reality itself is destabilizing. A strong directional light from the side enhances the transition, casting long shadows in the stable region and chaotic, flickering highlights in the collapsing region. Heat distortion visibly bends the air near the front, subtly warping the scene. Within the material, barely perceptible patterns resembling thermal maps and atmospheric currents are fused into the texture — not readable, only felt as embedded data. The background is nearly black, isolating the structure. Composition is asymmetrical, with the collapse dominating the foreground, forcing immediate emotional impact. No text, no symbols, no charts, no recognizable climate clichés. Ultra-realistic, cinematic, high contrast, volumetric lighting, shallow depth of field, hyper-detailed textures, magazine cover aesthetic, unsettling and urgent.
insight: Recent decades show dramatically accelerated global temperature increase compared to the stable past.
